architect.helpSubtitle: A separate AI workflow used before the game starts.

world.title: World Studio
world.subtitle: Build the world's lore and rules with the architect, or fill them in manually without starting a game chat.

architect.title: Build world lore
architect.helpNote: It asks questions and builds the world bible: laws, faith, history, regions, powers, secrets, and location-generation rules.
architect.placeholder: For example: a dark isekai about oaths, debtor gods, and living roads… (Enter to send)
architect.intro:
Describe the world freely, or give me a direction and I will build out the details.

Especially useful details:

1. Genre and mood.
2. The approximate size and population of the world.
3. Who inhabits it: people, ancestries, species, cultures, factions.
4. Which laws of reality apply: magic, technology, gods, death, roads.
5. What the world must contain.
6. What must not be added without a reason.
7. Which hidden truths only the GM should know.

We are not creating the opening scene, player role, or quest yet; those belong to the story step.

errors.imageMissingUrl: the sidecar did not return an image URL

machine.label: Machine post-apocalypse
machine.description: Ruins, autonomous nodes, water, energy, drones, and survivor communities.

values.title: Ashen Nexus
values.worldSize: One large region surrounding a chain of old machine hubs. The world extends far beyond it, but communications and roads are unreliable.
values.population: Hundreds of thousands of survivors divided among communities, caravan alliances, and machine cults.
values.publicPremise: People live around old water, energy, and repair hubs. The machines never vanished: some serve, while others guard protocols that no one understands anymore.

worldLore.world_laws:
Old machines follow protocols, not morality.
Water, energy, spare parts, and hub access matter more than coins.
worldLore.inhabitants:
Survivor communities
Machine cults
Caravaneers
Autonomous drones
worldLore.regions:
A network of hubs in the ash zone
Dry highways between settlements
Sealed machine sectors
worldLore.power_centers:
Water-community councils
Repair brotherhoods
Hub cults
worldLore.location_rules: Every location must reveal the cost of water, energy, spare parts, access, or signal.
worldLore.prohibited_elements: No classical magic unless it is explained as a malfunction, cult belief, or alien artifact.

isekai.label: Fantasy isekai
isekai.description: Oaths, local spirits, the price of magic, summoned outsiders, and local debts.

values.title: Threshold of the Second Sky
values.worldSize: A continent of several kingdoms, road spirits, and local shrines. The game can begin anywhere within it.
values.population: Tens of millions of people, spirit-blooded lineages, summoned outsiders, and minor folk.
values.publicPremise: Names, oaths, and debts carry the force of law and magic. Summoned outsiders appear rarely, but almost always become part of an ancient covenant.

worldLore.dogmas:
A name and an oath have both legal and mystical force.
Spirits of place remember debts better than people do.
worldLore.world_laws:
Magic requires a name, a price, or a recognized right.
Long journeys change rumors and the balance of power.
worldLore.inhabitants:
Ancestral houses
Spirits of place
Summoned outsiders
Runesmith guilds
worldLore.regions:
The Seven Lands beneath the Shattered Moon
Living roads
Threshold shrines
worldLore.religions:
The cult of road spirits
The official faith of oaths
worldLore.gods: The Elder Spirits of the Thresholds
worldLore.location_rules: Every new location must connect to a debt, authority, road, or spirit of place.
worldLore.prohibited_elements:
No free resurrection.
No magic without a name, price, or covenant.

frontier.label: The frontier
frontier.description: Roads, factions, rumors, ancient sites, and settlements with real functions.

values.title: Edge of the Old Roads
values.worldSize: One settled continent with kingdoms, frontier roads, ancient sites, and distant lands beyond the map.
values.population: Several million inhabitants: city-states, villages, nomadic families, road orders, and ancient peoples.
values.publicPremise: Roads matter more than walls: rumors, caravans, old agreements, and dangerous places bind settlements more tightly than any crown.

worldLore.conflicts:
The crown is trying to subjugate the roads.
Ancient places are awakening again.
Frontier settlements are fighting over tolls.
worldLore.regions:
Royal roads
Ancient forests
Frontier towns
Forgotten shrines
worldLore.power_centers:
Road orders
City councils
Caravan houses
The frontier guard
worldLore.economy:
Tolls
Caravans
Protection debts
Rights of passage
worldLore.location_rules: Every road must have a character, a price, a risk, and people who know its secrets.
worldLore.prohibited_elements: No random dungeons disconnected from roads, debts, or ancient law.

title.label: World name
title.placeholder: For example: Threshold of the Second Sky

worldSize.label: World size
worldSize.placeholder: For example: one continent; a school within a vast magical society; a galactic sector with dozens of planets.

population.label: Population
population.placeholder: For example: tens of millions, 5 sapient species, hundreds of cultures.

publicPremise.label: Public world description
publicPremise.placeholder: What can safely be told to the player without revealing the opening quest or GM secrets.

worldImage.promptPlaceholder: English prompt for a world overview image: how the world looks, key landscapes, settlements, peoples, magic/technology cues, mood.

worldMap.promptPlaceholder: English prompt for a readable world map: geography, regions, borders, routes, settlements, labels, cartography style.

lore.title: World bible
lore.hint: Fill it in yourself or wait for the architect. Put each item on a new line.

hiddenPremise.label: Hidden premise (GM secret)
hiddenPremise.placeholder: What only the GM knows and the player must not be told.

world.saveNote: A name, genre, tone, world size, population, and either a public description or world bible are required. Saving does not start a chat.

story.title: Story Studio
story.subtitle: Build a story premise on top of the world with the architect. The draft is saved automatically at every step.
story.subtitleWithWorld: Build a story premise in “{{world}}” with the architect. The draft is saved automatically at every step.

architect.title: Build the plot
architect.helpNote: It builds the premise of one story in an existing world: the story brief, public introduction, hidden GM truth, opening scene, NPCs, facts, and a suggested protagonist. No chapters, objectives, or endings—the engine does not track them yet.
architect.placeholder: For example: intrigue in a port city where inspectors vanish and the player is the new investigator… (Enter to send)
architect.intro:
Describe the story you want in this world, or give me a direction and I will build the premise.

Especially useful details:

1. What the story is about and what drives the player (story brief).
2. The public introduction—what the player sees and knows at the start.
3. The story's hidden truth (GM-only secret).
4. The opening scene: place, nearby characters, and the source of tension.
5. Who the player will meet (NPCs) and which facts the world already knows.
6. What the suggested protagonist might be like (the player can replace them at launch).

I only write the premise of one story on top of the world's canon—no chapters, objectives, or endings.

title.label: Story title
title.placeholder: For example: Inspection at Salt Port

description.label: Short description (for the story list)
description.placeholder: One line for the story list.

storyBrief.label: Player premise (story brief)
storyBrief.placeholder: Who the player is and what draws them into the story, in a few sentences.

publicIntro.label: Public introduction
publicIntro.placeholder: What the player sees and knows at the start, without GM secrets.

hiddenTruth.label: Hidden truth (GM secret)
hiddenTruth.placeholder: What lies behind the story and must not be revealed directly to the player.

time.label: Starting time (minutes after midnight; for example, 480 = 08:00)

protagonist.hint: Suggested protagonist (the player can replace them at launch).

background.label: Background (one line)
background.placeholder: What ties the hero to this story.

scene.hint: The opening scene—where the player enters the story.

title.label: Scene title
title.placeholder: For example: Gates of Salt Port

description.label: Scene description
description.placeholder: What the player sees at the start, in concrete sensory detail.

tension.label: Scene tension
tension.placeholder: What makes this a scene rather than a lobby.

lists.presentNpcs: NPCs in the scene (one id per line)

properNouns.label: Proper nouns (one per line)
properNouns.placeholder: Names that must be written consistently, one per line.

actions.saveProtagonistTitle: Save the story protagonist as a portable .gmchar package

story.saveNote: The draft is saved automatically after every architect response. A story brief and public introduction are required to launch. Launching opens the game chat for this story.

character.title: Character Studio
character.subtitle: Build a portable character sheet with the architect, or edit every field manually and save the sheet directly.

base.summaryAvailable: Base: {{bases}} — the architect uses {{scope}} public canon.
base.summaryMissing: Base: {{bases}} — the source material is unavailable; the architect will preserve the sheet's recorded links.

architect.title: Build a character
architect.helpNote: It builds one portable character sheet: name and role, abilities, skills, HP, inventory, equipment, and spells. Without a base, the hero is universal; with a base, the architect uses its public canon. The sheet remains portable either way, and the hero can be launched in another story (with a warning if the worlds differ).
architect.placeholder: For example: a stern half-elf ranger raised in the marshes, skilled with bows and herbs… (Enter to send)
architect.intro:
Describe the character you want, or give me a direction and I will build the sheet.

Especially useful details:

1. Name, role or class, and level.
2. Personality, values, background, and appearance.
3. Abilities (strength, dexterity, and so on) and key skills.
4. HP, armor class, equipment, and inventory.
5. Spells and slots, if the character uses them.

{{closing}}

closing.story: I will build a hero for the story “{{story}}” using its public premise, while keeping the sheet portable.
closing.world: I will build a hero for the world “{{world}}” using its public canon, while keeping the sheet portable.
closing.missing: The hero's base is recorded in the package, but its material is currently unavailable; I will preserve the sheet's existing links.
closing.standalone: I am building one portable character sheet that can be launched in any story.

appearance.placeholder: Build, species, and stable physical traits.

background.label: Background

personality.label: Personality
personality.placeholder: How the hero behaves and what drives them.

values.label: Values
values.placeholder: What they believe in and will not compromise.

gmNotes.label: Hero secret (GM-only; the public persona remains a legend)

character.saveNote: Edit the sheet manually and select “Save sheet,” or continue the conversation with the architect: the draft is sent with every message. A name is required to launch.
